text: Thank you for the opportunity to present on single-port robotic trans-anal TME or SP robotic taut TME. This is the first presentation ever of the single-port robot being utilized to perform taut TME. Here are my disclosures. Transanal TME surgery has been developed to help facilitate the difficult aspect of operating for low rectal cancers. Demonstrated here is utilizing a new technology, SP robot, to operate on a patient with a low rectal cancer at the five millimeter level.

The surgeon begins the operation by everting the anal canal and scoring the mucosa circumferentially with electrocautery. Scissors are then used to incise fully into the inner sphincteric plane, entering the plane between the internal and external sphincter. The rectum is freed circumferentially and closed with an imbricating, running, horizontal mattress purse-string suture using ovicral. The dissection is completed.

The gel point path is inserted and secured, followed by the docking of the single-port robot through a 2.5 cm trocar. The camera is docked first, and three robotic arms are visible: a grasper, scissors, and a bipolar grasper. The scissors are used to incise the plane between the mesorectum and areolar tissue, with dissection proceeding anteriorly and then posteriorly into the proper plane. The mesorectum is dissected upwards, demonstrating the 'holy plane' as described by Bill Heald.

The surgeon demonstrates three-arm retraction, dissecting the areolar tissue plane posteriorly over the sacrum. The camera lens is moved to dissect the lateral aspect of the mesorectum. The single-team approach is shown, with a holographic image visible at six o'clock on the screen. The dissection proceeds anteriorly, separating the perineum from the mesorectum and approaching the pouch of Douglas from below. The pouch of Douglas is opened, with the perineum fluttering as air insufflates the abdominal cavity, revealing the sigmoid colon in the pelvis.

The camera is repositioned posteriorly to continue the dissection, utilizing a holographic image to prevent robotic arm conflicts. Short bursts of energy are applied to dissect between the mesorectum and endopelvic fascia, revealing the embryologic fusion plane. The dissection progresses over the sacrum, taking the endopelvic fascia with it. The mesorectal dissection is completed from below, and the rectum is delivered into the abdominal cavity through the opened pouch of Douglas. The dissection is finished first on the left side, then on the right side, moving up the parorectal sulcus. The operation concludes with a view into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon completes the trans-anal portion of the procedure, undocks the robot, and places a single port at the future ileostomy site. Using a flexible tip camera, the abdomen is explored, and the remaining attachments of the total mesorectal excision (TME) are finished. The rectum is delivered into the abdominal cavity, and the bare pelvis is inspected following the complete TME resection. The specimen is delivered transanally, and a hand-sewn colonic anastomosis is performed with full thickness bites at twelve, six, three, and nine o'clock positions. The anastomosis is tied, and a loop ileostomy is created for diversion.
